skill.name: error-detective
skill.description: Systematic debugging skill for analyzing errors, stack traces, logs, and root causes using the TRACE framework.
skill.summary: Use TRACE to investigate errors, isolate root causes, and verify fixes.
skill.author: AutumnsGrove

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, weak-crypto, and heuristic issues, but most are false positives from Markdown examples and stack trace samples. The confirmed risk is that a bundled Python helper reads user-supplied files and writes local debug session files, so publication is acceptable with a filesystem and script-use warning.

medium_findings.title: Local File Read and Log Exposure Risk
medium_findings.description: The helper script accepts a file path and reads stack traces or logs from that path. This is intended debugging behavior, but agents should avoid running it on secrets, credentials, or unrelated private files because matching log lines can be printed in output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly opens user-supplied paths and prints parsed results. No malicious exfiltration was found, but the data exposure risk is real in debugging workflows.

medium_findings.title: Local Debug Session Files Are Written
medium_findings.description: The debug session manager creates a .debug_sessions directory and writes current and archived session JSON files. This is useful for debugging, but notes may contain sensitive incident details if users paste secrets into session descriptions or notes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are explicit and semantically tied to user-provided debugging notes. The behavior is legitimate, but sensitive local persistence is plausible.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are fenced Markdown examples or usage instructions, not code executed by the skill. Examples include running the helper script and git bisect commands for debugging.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are documentation code fences. They can guide a user or agent to run commands, but there is no hidden shell execution in these Markdown files.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The fetch calls and hardcoded URLs are examples for diagnosing API failures and CORS behavior. No executable network client exists in the helper script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is sample JavaScript and explanatory CORS text. The reviewed Python script imports no network libraries and performs no outbound requests.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm findings, but review found no confirmed cryptographic implementation or credential handling behavior in the executable helper. The alerts appear to be keyword matches in debugging documentation and examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review did not find md5, sha1, password hashing, encryption, or secret transport code. Confidence is high enough to dismiss, but the static line labels were broad.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied Path File Reads
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI reads paths supplied to parse-trace and analyze-log. This should be limited to intended debugging files and reviewed before use on production logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path arguments flow directly into open calls. The behavior is transparent and user-driven, but it is still a sensitive pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Local Debug Notes
dangerous_patterns.description: Session descriptions, notes, and solutions are saved under .debug_sessions. Sensitive values in those notes may remain on disk after the debugging session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code creates local session files and serializes user-provided text. This is intentional but should be disclosed.

content.value_statement: Complex errors are hard to triage when stack traces, logs, and symptoms point in different directions. This skill provides a structured TRACE workflow, examples, and helper utilities for root cause analysis.
actual_capabilities: Guides error investigations with the TRACE framework: Trace, Read, Analyze, Check, Execute.
Explains stack traces and separates root causes from secondary symptoms.
Provides examples for Python, JavaScript, Java, Go, database, and API errors.
Includes a Python helper for parsing stack traces and summarizing log errors.
Supports local debug session notes with timestamps and closure summaries.
Suggests verification steps and regression prevention after fixes.
limitations: It does not automatically fix code without user or agent implementation work.
The helper script reads local files selected by the user and may expose log content in output.
Examples are general patterns and may not match every framework version.
It does not replace production observability, incident response, or security review.

use_cases.title: Triage a Failing Feature
use_cases.description: Use the TRACE checklist to capture the full error, inspect the failing code path, and confirm the root cause before editing.

use_cases.title: Investigate Production Logs
use_cases.description: Analyze error frequency, repeated messages, timestamps, and likely failure classes from application logs.

use_cases.title: Teach Debugging Process
use_cases.description: Give teams a repeatable workflow for stack traces, hypotheses, verification, and prevention notes.

prompt_templates.title: Explain This Error
prompt_templates.prompt: Use error-detective to explain this error in plain language. Identify the error type, likely cause, failing line, and first safe check. Here is the error: [paste error].

prompt_templates.title: Apply TRACE to a Bug
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the TRACE framework for this bug. List what to collect, what the error says, what context to inspect, root cause hypotheses, and verification steps. Bug details: [describe bug].

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Logs Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use error-detective to analyze these sanitized log excerpts. Summarize repeated errors, timestamps, probable root causes, and next tests. Do not reveal or store secrets. Logs: [paste sanitized logs].

prompt_templates.title: Plan and Verify a Fix
prompt_templates.prompt: Use error-detective to build a fix plan for this incident. Include root cause evidence, rejected hypotheses, minimal code changes, tests, rollback concerns, and prevention measures. Context: [paste details].

output_examples.input: A Python AttributeError says a NoneType object has no attribute profile.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies the failing object, traces where it should be initialized, proposes checks for missing database rows or API nulls, and recommends a regression test.

output_examples.input: A cart update fails when an API sometimes returns a server error.
output_examples.output: The skill separates client-side symptoms from backend timeout causes, recommends response status checks, and lists verification steps for retries and user messaging.

output_examples.input: A production log contains repeated ERROR and WARN messages across a time window.
output_examples.output: The skill groups repeated messages, highlights first and last timestamps, ranks likely causes, and suggests focused follow-up checks.

best_practices: Sanitize logs and stack traces before sharing them with an AI assistant.
Capture the complete error and reproduction steps before changing code.
Verify the root cause with tests or controlled checks before closing the issue.
anti_patterns: Do not paste credentials, tokens, customer data, or private production logs into debug notes.
Do not run helper scripts on unrelated system files or secret stores.
Do not apply a quick fix that only hides the symptom without testing the root cause.

faq.question: What does this skill help debug?
faq.answer: It helps investigate exceptions, stack traces, log errors, failed requests, configuration issues, and intermittent failures.

faq.question: Does it change my code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides analysis and fix planning. Any code changes must be made separately by the user or agent.

faq.question: What is the TRACE framework?
faq.answer: TRACE means Trace, Read, Analyze, Check, and Execute. It is a structured process for debugging.

faq.question: Can it analyze logs?
faq.answer: Yes. The included helper script can summarize local log files and repeated error patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe for production incidents?
faq.answer: Use sanitized data. The helper reads local files and debug notes may be saved on disk.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
